Which of the following confirms the diagnosis of patient suspected to have Diptheritic Laryngitis
A. Bacterial smear and culture from the membrane
B. Direct laryngoscopy
C. All of the above
D. Xray of the neck
C. All of the above

The incubation period for Hepatitis B is
A. 15-19 days
B. 60-180 days
C. 14-160 days
D. 21-63 days
B. 60-180 days

Which of the following stage of Chronic Kidney
Disease has moderate decrease in GFR?
A. Stage 5
B. Stage 1
C. Stage 4
D. Stage 2
E. Stage 3
E. Stage 3

A 6 months old male infant was seen at your clinic for
watery stools a day PTC. A stool culture is considered
in the following cases:
A. during outbreaks
B. all of the above
C. Bloody diarrhea
D. stool with fecal leucocytes
B. all of the above

A severe dengue patient came in the ER due to
severe respiratory distress and in need of advanced
ventilation. Upon intubation noted with bloody
secretions in the airway. What is the next step in the
management of this patient?
A. Insert IV line and give vasopressor
B. Maintain positive pressure ventilation
C. Provide surfactant via ET tube
D. Secure blood products for possible
transfusion

B. Maintain positive pressure ventilation

The presence of bloody secretions in the airway suggests that the patient may have developed bleeding tendencies or hemorrhagic manifestations. In such cases, maintaining adequate oxygenation and ventilation is crucial. Ventilation can be accomplished via endotracheal intubation and mechanical ventilation. The other options mentioned (IV line and vasopressor, surfactant via ET tube, blood products for transfusion) may be considered as part of the overall management plan but are not the immediate next step in this scenario.

All of the following is/are hallmark of Nephrotic syndrome, except
A. edema
B. hyperlipidemia
C. hypoalbuminemia
D. Heavy proteinuria
E. HPN

E. HPN (High Blood Pressure)

Nephrotic syndrome is a condition that results from damage to the glomeruli in the kidneys, leading to excessive protein loss in the urine, low levels of albumin in the blood, and accumulation of fluid in tissues (edema). Hyperlipidemia and heavy proteinuria are also characteristic features of nephrotic syndrome. However, hypertension (HPN) is not a hallmark of nephrotic syndrome, although it may be present in some cases due to renal impairment and fluid overload.

Which of the following is/are common cause of
persistent proteinuria among school age children?
A. All of the above
B. Orthostatic proteinuria
C. Fixed proteinuria
D. Transient proteinuria

B. Orthostatic proteinuria

Orthostatic proteinuria is a common cause of persistent proteinuria among school-age children. It is a benign condition characterized by intermittent proteinuria that occurs during the day when the child is in an upright position, but disappears at night when the child is lying down. Fixed proteinuria refers to persistent proteinuria that is present at all times and is suggestive of an underlying renal disease. Transient proteinuria is a temporary condition that is usually caused by a non-renal illness or infection and resolves once the underlying cause is treated. Therefore, options C and D are not common causes of persistent proteinuria among school-age children.

A 10 yo male child with fever and cough was brought
to your clinic. History revealed he was exposed to his
grandmother with TB.Work up done :Tuberculin test
(+), chest xray (-), Direct sputum smear
microscopy(-).Most likely he has
A. TB Disease
B. TB Infection
C. PCAP
D. TB Exposure

B. TB Infection

The child has a positive tuberculin test in the context of a recent exposure to someone with TB, suggesting that he has been infected with the TB bacteria. The negative chest x-ray and direct sputum smear microscopy make it unlikely that he has active TB disease. PCAP (pneumonia, cough, and fever) is a non-specific presentation that can occur with a variety of respiratory infections and illnesses, including TB. TB exposure (option D) refers to exposure to someone with TB and does not imply that the child has the infection. Therefore, the most likely diagnosis in this case is TB infection.

In the following human pedigrees, the filled symbols
represent the affected individuals. You may assume
that the disease allele is rare and therefore individuals
marrying into the family are unlikely to have defective
allele.What is the most likely mode of inheritance for
this pedigree?

It is difficult to answer this question without the actual pedigree diagram. Please provide the pedigree or a description of the pedigree for us to be able to determine the most likely mode of inheritance.

Some carriers of an X-linked recessive condition can
manifest the condition because:
A. They have a dominant mutation
B. Of random X-inactivation
C. They are homozygous for the mutation
D. Of skewed X-inactivation

D. Of skewed X-inactivation

X-linked inheritance refers to conditions that are caused by mutations on genes located on the X chromosome. In females, who have two copies of the X chromosome, X-linked recessive conditions are usually masked by the fact that they have a normal copy of the gene on the other X chromosome. However, in some cases, females who carry the mutant allele on one of their X chromosomes may still manifest the condition due to skewed X-inactivation. This refers to the inactivation of one X chromosome in each cell during early development, resulting in the expression of genes from only one of the X chromosomes in most cells. If the mutant allele on the inactivated X chromosome is present in a higher proportion of cells, it can lead to disease manifestation even in carrier females. Therefore, option D is the correct answer. Options A, B, and C do not apply to X-linked inheritance.

Some carriers of an X-linked recessive condition can manifest the condition because:
A. They have a dominant mutation
B. Of random X-inactivation
C. They are homozygous for the mutation
D. Of skewed X-inactivation

D. Of skewed X-inactivation

X-linked inheritance refers to conditions that are caused by mutations on genes located on the X chromosome. In females, who have two copies of the X chromosome, X-linked recessive conditions are usually masked by the fact that they have a normal copy of the gene on the other X chromosome. However, in some cases, females who carry the mutant allele on one of their X chromosomes may still manifest the condition due to skewed X-inactivation. This refers to the inactivation of one X chromosome in each cell during early development, resulting in the expression of genes from only one of the X chromosomes in most cells. If the mutant allele on the inactivated X chromosome is present in a higher proportion of cells, it can lead to disease manifestation even in carrier females. Therefore, option D is the correct answer. Options A, B, and C do not apply to X-linked inheritance.

A 15 yo female adolescent with low grade fever and
cough with nasal congestion and purulent nasal
discharge for more than a week. Most likely she has:
A. Common Colds
B. Acute Sinusitis
C. Sub acute Sinusitis
D. Chronic Sinusitis

B. Acute Sinusitis

The symptoms described by the patient, i.e., low-grade fever, cough, nasal congestion, and purulent nasal discharge, suggest a diagnosis of acute sinusitis. Acute sinusitis is a common condition in which the sinuses become inflamed and swollen due to an infection, usually a viral or bacterial respiratory infection. It is characterized by the sudden onset of symptoms that last for less than 4 weeks. Subacute sinusitis typically lasts for 4-12 weeks, and chronic sinusitis lasts for more than 12 weeks. Common colds (option A) are a milder form of respiratory infection that typically present with a runny or stuffy nose, sneezing, sore throat, and mild fever. Therefore, option B is the best answer in this case.

An 8-months old infant with diagnosis of high risk
pneumonia from a referring hospital came in at the
emergency room because of severe respiratory
symptoms How do you describe an infant in
impending respiratory failure that requires an
immediate respiratory resuscitation?
A. Increase respiratory effort but able drink
water
B. Seesaw breathing with decrease level of
sensorium
C. Increase respiratory rate with chest
indrawings
D. Awake but irritable infant when approach

C. Increase respiratory rate with chest indrawings

An infant in impending respiratory failure may present with an increase in respiratory rate, along with chest indrawings, indicating increased effort to breathe due to respiratory distress. This is often accompanied by grunting, flaring of the nostrils, and possible cyanosis (blue tint to the skin). Seesaw breathing (option B), which describes the paradoxical inward movement of the abdomen during inspiration and outward movement during expiration, is a sign of upper airway obstruction and severe respiratory distress. An awake but irritable infant (option D) may be a sign of respiratory distress, but it is not specific enough to indicate impending respiratory failure. The ability to drink water (option A) is not a reliable indicator of respiratory status and cannot be used to determine the need for respiratory resuscitation. Therefore, option C is the most appropriate answer.

An 8-months old infant with diagnosis of high risk pneumonia from a referring hospital came in at the emergency room because of severe respiratory symptoms How do you describe an infant in impending respiratory failure that requires an immediate respiratory resuscitation?
A. Increase respiratory effort but able drink water
B. Seesaw breathing with decrease level of sensorium
C. Increase respiratory rate with chest indrawings
D. Awake but irritable infant when approach

C. Increase respiratory rate with chest indrawings

An infant in impending respiratory failure may present with an increase in respiratory rate, along with chest indrawings, indicating increased effort to breathe due to respiratory distress. This is often accompanied by grunting, flaring of the nostrils, and possible cyanosis (blue tint to the skin). Seesaw breathing (option B), which describes the paradoxical inward movement of the abdomen during inspiration and outward movement during expiration, is a sign of upper airway obstruction and severe respiratory distress. An awake but irritable infant (option D) may be a sign of respiratory distress, but it is not specific enough to indicate impending respiratory failure. The ability to drink water (option A) is not a reliable indicator of respiratory status and cannot be used to determine the need for respiratory resuscitation. Therefore, option C is the most appropriate answer.
why option b is wrong

Option B refers to seesaw breathing with decreased level of sensorium as a descriptor of an infant in respiratory failure requiring immediate resuscitation. However, this is an inaccurate and potentially misleading description. 

Seesaw breathing is a type of breathing pattern characterized by the inward movement of the abdomen during inspiration and the outward movement during expiration. This pattern typically suggests upper airway obstruction and severe respiratory distress, but it is not necessarily indicative of impending respiratory failure. 

Decreased level of sensorium, on the other hand, refers to a state of altered consciousness or reduced awareness, which can be caused by a variety of factors including respiratory failure, hypoxia, and other medical conditions. It is not a specific sign of respiratory distress, nor is it a reliable indicator that immediate respiratory resuscitation is required.

Therefore, while seesaw breathing and decreased level of sensorium can be present in an infant with respiratory distress, they are not reliable or specific enough indicators to suggest that immediate respiratory resuscitation is required. Instead, an increase in respiratory rate with chest indrawings (option C) is a more appropriate and specific sign of impending respiratory failure in this scenario.
give me the management for Pyoderma Purpuric Dermatosis

Pyoderma Purpuric Dermatosis (PPD), also known as Schamberg's disease, is a rare condition characterized by red-to-purple colored patches and bumps on the skin, usually affecting the legs and feet. The exact cause of PPD is not fully understood, but it is believed to be related to a hypersensitivity reaction to some immune triggers or infections. There is no specific treatment for PPD, but the following measures may help in managing the condition:

1. Symptomatic treatment: This includes the use of topical corticosteroids or oral antihistamines to help relieve symptoms like itching, redness, and swelling of the affected skin.

2. Avoidance of triggers: Patients with PPD should avoid exposure to known triggers of the condition, such as certain drugs and viral infections.

3. Support stockings: Wearing compression stockings may help improve blood circulation and reduce the swelling in the affected legs and feet.

4. Immune system modulators: Although not commonly used, medications that suppress the immune system, such as systemic corticosteroids, can be prescribed in severe cases of PPD to suppress inflammation.

5. Follow-up: Patients with PPD should be monitored regularly to track the progress of the disease and ensure that no complications arise.

It is important to note that PPD is a benign condition that usually resolves on its own over several weeks to months. However, in rare cases, the condition may recur or progress to affect other parts of the body, in which case the patient may need further evaluation and treatment.
what causes pyoderma Purpuric Dermatosis

The exact cause of Pyoderma Purpuric Dermatosis (PPD) or Schamberg's Disease is not fully understood, but it is believed to be related to a hypersensitivity reaction to some immune triggers or infections. 

PPD is thought to be caused by immune complex deposition in the skin. Immune complexes are formed when antibodies in the blood combine with foreign substances, such as bacterial or viral antigens, drugs, or other environmental factors. These immune complexes can deposit in the small blood vessels of the skin, causing inflammation and damage to the blood vessel walls. This leads to the characteristic red-to-purple colored patches and bumps on the skin, usually affecting the legs and feet.

In some cases, PPD is associated with viral infections, such as hepatitis B and C, HIV, and parvovirus B19. It can also be triggered by certain medications, such as nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), antibiotics, and anticoagulants. 

However, in many cases, no specific trigger can be identified. The condition is more common in males and typically affects adults in their middle age. It is not contagious and does not run in families.
give me the management for Pigmented Purpuric Dermatosis

Pigmented Purpuric Dermatosis (PPD) is a chronic skin condition that is characterized by red-to-brown colored patches or spots on the skin, usually on the lower legs, thighs, and buttocks. The exact cause of the condition is unknown, but it is believed to be related to capillary fragility and some immune reactions. There is no specific treatment for PPD, but the following measures may help in managing the condition:

1. Symptomatic treatment: This includes the use of topical corticosteroids or oral antihistamines to help relieve symptoms like itching, redness, and swelling of the affected skin.

2. Avoidance of triggers: Patients with PPD should avoid exposure to known triggers of the condition, such as certain drugs, topical irritants, and prolonged standing or sitting.

3. Tight-fitting compression stockings: Wearing compression stockings can help improve blood flow and reduce swelling in the affected areas.

4. Laser therapy: Some patients may benefit from laser therapy, which can help reduce redness and improve the appearance of the affected skin.

5. Follow-up: Patients with PPD should be monitored regularly to track the progress of the disease and to ensure that no complications arise.

It is important to note that PPD is a chronic, benign condition that may recur over time. Patients with severe or recurrent PPD may require additional evaluation and treatment, such as immunosuppressive therapy or phototherapy. The choice of treatment depends on the severity of the condition, the age and health status of the patient, and other individual factors that should be discussed with a healthcare provider.
give me examples of ymptomatic treatment

Symptomatic treatment refers to treatments that are aimed at relieving the symptoms of a disease or condition rather than addressing the underlying cause. Here are some examples of symptomatic treatments for various conditions:

1. Pain relief medications, such as acetaminophen, ibuprofen, and opioids, are used to alleviate pain in conditions such as arthritis, back pain, and headaches.

2. Antipyretics, such as acetaminophen and aspirin, are used to reduce fever in conditions such as flu, pneumonia, and other infections.

3. Antihistamines, such as loratadine or diphenhydramine, are used to reduce itching and redness in conditions such as allergic reactions, hives, and eczema.

4. Topical steroids, such as hydrocortisone cream or ointment, are used to reduce inflammation, redness, and itching in skin conditions such as eczema, dermatitis, and psoriasis.

5. Antiemetics, such as metoclopramide or ondansetron, are used to relieve nausea and vomiting in conditions such as gastroenteritis, chemotherapy, and motion sickness.

6. Decongestants, such as pseudoephedrine or phenylephrine, are used to relieve nasal congestion in conditions such as colds, sinusitis, and allergies.

It is important to note that symptomatic treatments may help to relieve the symptoms of a condition, but they do not treat the underlying cause or cure the condition. Therefore, it is important to work with a healthcare provider to determine the best course of treatment based on the underlying cause of the symptoms.
